Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Executive Summary

  • Interest rate tightening: A net 43.8% of Slovak firms reported tighter bank loan interest rates, up from a net 5.5% — a 38.3 pp reversal and the sharpest reading in four waves.
  • Macro drag, not banks: Bank willingness to lend held at a net 7.8% improvement, so the cost and access squeeze reflects rates and a deteriorating economic outlook, adverse for a net 51.6% of firms.
  • Margin squeeze persists: Labour costs kept rising for a net 79% of firms, 19.85 pp above the EA, while turnover still fell for a net 13% despite improving 13.39 pp.
  • Credit line strain: The Slovak credit line gap held at +15.3 pp, 1.3 pp above the EA, as need rose to +15.2 pp while availability stayed near flat.

Financing Conditions

Interest rate tightening for Slovak firms sharply accelerated in wave 39

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10) ⓘ SQL

  • A net 43.8% of Slovak firms (n=76) reported tighter bank loan interest rates, rising sharply from a net 5.5% (n=62) in the prior quarter — a 38.3 pp reversal, implying significant upward pressure on borrowing costs.
  • Available loan or credit line size tightened for a net 12.4% of Slovak firms (n=74), up from a net 6.1% in wave 38, exceeding the EA's net 9.1%, signalling reduced access to financing.
  • Non-interest financing costs were unreportable for Slovakia in wave 39, while the EA measure eased by 6.9 pp to a net 29.7% (n=1,894) — leaving Slovak firms' cost structure opaque.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Credit line stress persists as Slovak bank loan conditions ease

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9) ⓘ SQL

  • The credit line gap for Slovak firms held at +15.3pp in wave 39, 1.3pp above the EA's +14.0pp.
  • The bank loan gap widened to -6.0pp (from -4.0pp in the prior wave) for Slovak firms (n=214), driven by availability improving to +5.6pp, leaving Slovakia 8.8pp less stressed than the EA.
  • The trade credit gap reversed from +6.3pp to -1.7pp for Slovak firms (n=180), as need fell from +7.1pp to -3.4pp, placing Slovakia 12.7pp less stressed than the EA's +11.0pp — easing short-term payment pressures.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak bank loan availability expectations diverged from improving EA trend

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23) ⓘ SQL

  • A net 6% of Slovak firms (n=104) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate in wave 39, worsening 1.3 pp from the previous wave—leaving Slovak firms more pessimistic than their EA peers.
  • Slovak trade credit expectations improved by 4.0 pp to a net 6% expecting deterioration (n=90), moving counter to the EA, where expectations edged 1.0 pp more adverse—signalling tighter financing conditions locally.
  • Credit line availability expectations improved for both Slovak firms (by 3.2 pp to a net 4%, n=136) and EA firms (by 1.5 pp to a net 2%)—reducing but not closing the gap between regions.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms shifted toward fixed investment while working capital needs stayed dominant

Purpose of Financing (Q6A) ⓘ SQL

  • Fixed investment financing rose from 34.2% to 40.7% among Slovak firms (n=242), a 6.5 pp increase that lifted SK above the EA average of 38.4% — suggesting stronger growth-oriented investment.
  • Inventory and working capital remained the most-cited purpose at 57.0% (n=242), 16.2 pp above the EA average of 40.8%, continuing a persistent structural gap — highlighting ongoing liquidity constraints.
  • Refinancing or paying off obligations declined by 4.5 pp to 17.0% (n=241), converging toward the EA rate of 15.8% — indicating reduced debt-servicing pressure.

Bank willingness stable but economic outlook weighs on Slovak firms

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11) ⓘ SQL

  • A net 7.8% of Slovak firms (n=310) reported improved bank willingness to lend, unchanged from wave 38 and above both the EA's net 5.6% and Germany's net -4.2% — suggesting stable credit supply conditions.
  • The general economic outlook deteriorated for a net 51.6% of Slovak firms (n=472), worsening by 7.2 pp from wave 38's net 44.4% — 22.2 pp more adverse than the EA's net 29.4%, putting pressure on financing access.
  • Access to public financial support improved by 12.6 pp to a net 21.2% of firms reporting deterioration (n=310), compared with a net 33.8% in wave 38, though conditions remain negative.

Economic Situation of Firms

Slovak turnover remains negative despite 13 pp improvement in wave 39

Business Situation Indicators (Q2) ⓘ SQL

  • A net 13% of Slovak firms (n=242) reported falling turnover in wave 39, improving by 13.39 pp from the prior quarter's net 26%—yet still trailing the EA's net 8% increase.
  • A net 34% of Slovak firms (n=242) reported declining profits, improving 5.42 pp from a net 40% in wave 38—compared with a net 15% reporting profit declines across the EA.
  • Labour costs continued to rise for a net 79% of Slovak firms (n=242), easing 4.81 pp from the prior quarter but remaining 19.85 pp above the EA's net 59%—adding pressure on margins.

Slovak turnover expectations fell while euro area outlook improved

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26) ⓘ SQL

  • A net 6.75% of Slovak firms (n=228) expected turnover to rise over the coming quarters, falling 14.15 pp from a net 20.90% in the prior wave, against the euro area's net 25.88% (n=5,266) — signalling weaker domestic demand.
  • Slovak fixed investment expectations rose to a net 14.67% (n=228), up 3.88 pp from the prior wave, outpacing the euro area's net 9.45%, which declined 3.13 pp — suggesting firms may prioritise cost-cutting over growth.
  • The gap between Slovak and euro area turnover expectations widened to 19.13 pp in wave 38, with the two economies moving in opposite directions — highlighting diverging business sentiment trends.

Slovak firms face margin compression as cost expectations outpace selling prices

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34) ⓘ SQL

  • Slovak firms (n=224) anticipated non-labour input price rises of 7.33% over the next 12 months, against selling price expectations of only 3.75% (n=222) — squeezing margins further.
  • Wage expectations remained at 4.12% (n=233) — easing just 0.09 pp from the prior wave — outpacing the EA's 2.60% by 1.52 pp and sustaining margin compression pressure.
  • Slovak 12-month inflation expectations eased 0.29 pp to 4.68% (n=193), remaining 0.80 pp above the EA average of 3.88% — adding to cost pressures.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms' upside inflation risk balance widened further above the euro area

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33) ⓘ SQL

  • A net 61.23% of Slovak firms (n=242) saw upside risks to five-year inflation expectations in wave 39, up 2.30 pp from a net 58.93% in wave 38 — suggesting firms anticipate persistent price pressures.
  • The SK–EA gap widened to 7.2 pp in wave 39, as Slovak firms' balance rose while the euro area's eased 1.28 pp to a net 54.04% (n=5,087) — highlighting diverging inflation concerns.
  • Euro area firms' upside inflation risk balance edged down from a net 55.32% in wave 38, placing Slovakia as the more inflation-risk-concerned economy.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Skilled staff shortages overtake costs as Slovak firms' top concern

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B) ⓘ SQL

  • Skilled staff availability ranked most pressing among the seven problems tested, scoring 6.64/10 (n=239), rising from 6.07 in Wave 38 — the largest increase across all categories, signalling heightened urgency for firms.
  • Costs of production or labour scored 6.62/10 (n=240), up from 6.19 in Wave 38, broadly in line with the euro area average of 6.74 — underscoring persistent cost pressures despite lower ranking.
  • Regulation scored 6.10/10 (n=236), easing marginally from 6.18 in the prior wave; access to finance remained below 5/10 and thus not a pressing concern.

Source: ECB SAFE microdata. Net balance = % reporting increase minus % reporting decrease. Positive = tightening/rising (adverse for firms unless noted). Negative = easing/falling.
